entry.content: SF take rule for absences where just before holiday and weekend, leaves are not allowed. Also across weekends leaves are not allowed.

entry.title: SSO implementation in GROW with SAP using Microsoft Entra ID
entry.content:
The SAP Single Sign-On application enables users to log in once to gain secure access to all the software they require throughout the day with no need to log in again ,with SAP Single Sign-On

There are several process to set up setup SSO in On premise system as well as on cloud, here we will discuss SSO setup between Azure and SAP on Public Cloud using IdP

There are two primary options in which SAP Identity Authentication Services and Microsoft Entra ID can be integrated:

  • Microsoft Entra ID as the Identity Provider (IdP): This scenario makes Microsoft Entra ID the central authentication hub, with users logging into SAP applications using their Microsoft Entra ID credentials.

 

  • SAP IAS as the IdP: In this case, SAP IAS becomes the primary authentication source, with users logging into Microsoft Entra ID applications using their SAP credentials.

Recently I got opportunity to setup SSO between Microsoft Entra ID (formerly known as Microsoft Azure Active Directory or Azure AD) , SAP IAS and SuccessFactors on SAP on Public Cloud (Grow with SAP) using SAP IAS as the IdP.

 

Below are step to configure SSO between Azure ,IAS and Success Factor.

1- Configure SAP IAS

Login on IAS system go to Applications and Resources -> Tenant Settings ->Single Sign On-> SAML 2.0 Configuration the  download metadata file on you system share this file to Azure team and ask for Federation Metadata XML 

 

 

 


2- Configure Microsoft Entra ID

This setp should be perform by azure admin system by following step

Create an Application in Microsoft Entra ID: This application represents your SAP IAS instance. Login to https://portal.azure.com and setup the Microsoft Entra ID.

 



Click Add -> Enterprise Applications 



  •  

 


By default, Microsoft Azure supports variety of applications. Search with SAP Cloud Identity Services. Select the SAP Cloud Identity Services and click on create.

 


We will be using the SAML Metadata file to setup the trust between Microsoft Entra ID and SAP Identity Authentication service (IAS). Click on Setup Single Sign-On.

 


 

Choose SAML as the SSO method and upload the SAP IAS metadata file.

 



 


After saving the application you can download the Federation Metadata XML file which we will add to the SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS).

 

3- Configure Federation metadata on  IAS

Login on IAS system and click identity Provider  and select Corporate identity Provider

 

Click on create

 

 

Fill are require data

 

 

Click on create it will create entry in Identity provider

 

 

 Now click on SAML2.0 Configuration

 

And upload azure xml file

 

 

 

 

All setting between Azure and IAS system has been done.You can check  SSO connection via login on IAS system ,it will pick Azure authentication and will login without asking password on IAS system

Now going to setup SSO connection between IAS and managed application system here I will setup connection between IAS to success factor, you can choose other system depend on requirement

Login on IAS system and click on Application & Resources and select SuccessFactors system

 

Now  select single Sign-on  and check Subject Name Identifier it should be as below

 

 

 

 

select single Sign-on  and check Default Name ID Format

 

 

Check attribute it should be as below

 

 

Now maintain domain in Conditional Authentication as per below

 

 

 

All setting has been completed for SF system

 

 

 

 

Now login to sf provision url and enable SSO

 

 

 

 

Now test sso from SF system it will automatically login on system without login page

 

Reference

  • Simplify SSO with Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) & ... - SAP Community
  • https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/entra/identity/saas-apps/sap-hana-cloud-platform-identity-authentication-tutorial

entry.title: Live After Go Live: Building for Success with Tech Implementations
entry.content:
Without a clear roadmap and a comprehensive approach, many businesses find it challenging to integrate and fully leverage new technologies. In my daily conversations with customers globally, I encountered over 200 organizations last year that were struggling to achieve desired results from their tech implementations and couldn’t easily identify what wasn’t working. I increasingly notice that organizations acquire technology with the hope of creating a new reality within their businesses. They have “the dream” in mind but fail to translate that dream into concrete objectives and then communicate those objectives effectively to the implementation team and the subsequent "sustainment team." Why do organizations invest so much effort in the implementation phase, only to neglect the post-go-live or adoption phase? Once the initial phase is complete, the visibility, change management, and project team meetings often dwindle. I frequently advise that the post-go-live phase is just as important, if not more so, than the implementation phase. It's the moment of truth: if the technology is used as intended and the new way of working is adopted, then the expected results can materialize.

What Needs to Happen? Organizations need to form a collaborative team that includes representatives from IT, the business area where the tech was implemented (e.g., HR, Procurement, Finance, Production), and, in many cases, the implementation partner. This team should:

  1. Understand Post-Implementation Activities: Be clear about the activities required to keep the technology relevant, updated, and aligned with evolving business needs.
  2. Define Roles and Responsibilities: Have clear and formalized roles and responsibilities for each team member.
  3. Allocate Time Appropriately: Ensure that resources are allocated appropriately, with some team members dedicating 100% of their time and others contributing less.
  4. Set Clear Objectives & KPIs: Develop clear objectives and key performance indicators (KPIs) that address the question, "What do we aim to achieve in this business area with the technology enabling results and experiences?"
  5. Create an Adoption Plan: Develop an adoption plan that includes change management activities, adoption metrics, and a method to report progress.

Executive Sponsorship  plays a crucial role in this process. Without one or more leaders at the highest level of the organization communicating the importance of the new technology and the new way of working, it becomes much harder to achieve widespread adoption. Leaders must send clear messages that the technology is here to stay, there is no Plan B, and everyone will receive necessary information and trainings to cope with the change but no joiners can not be accepted .

SAP Customers: Join the Conversation If you are an SAP customer and want to delve deeper into these topics, come to our 1:Many or 1:Few sessions where customers and experts from SAP discuss these critical issues. By focusing on the post-go-live phase with the same intensity as the implementation phase, organizations can ensure that their investments in technology yield the desired transformational outcomes.

Link  to 1:Many calendar

Link to customer 1:Few roundtables calendar

 @nwright @fry @NPARDO;

entry.title: Hands-on Tutorial: Building an AI Agent with human-in-the-loop control
entry.content:
Agentic AI is the start of a new era for business productivity. Using the Agent Builder in Joule Studio, businesses will be able to leverage AI agents to their fullest potential.

"While many in the software industry talk about AI agents these days, I can assure you, Joule will be the champion of them all. So far, we have added over 500 skills to Joule and we are well on track to cover 80% of the most frequent business and analytical transactions by the end of this year." - Christian Klein

This quote illustrates the position of Joule Studio as an absolute frontrunner especially when it comes to real world value.
The Agent builder will enable all employees regardless of technical background to harness the power of agentic AI grounded entirely within the business process.

Purpose of this blog

While Joule Studio is the absolute powerhouse for agentic AI, in this blog I want to illustrate how you can build a simple code-based agent that integrates into the BTP Services with tools that are available today. Such a code-based agent is highly customizable and can be tailored exactly to your needs.
For this purpose I will use the Python framework LangGraph, the Generative AI Hub SDK and the SuccessFactors Employee Central API. I will walk you through the implementation steps and provide as much background knowledge as necessary to understand this simple example

Use case

The use case for this demo is that of an agent dedicated to assist the user with their timesheet management.
This is a scenario where llm-based agents shine as we need to process natural language input. Additionally dynamic decision-making is required, albeit to a limited extent, e.g. the agent needs to decide on the fly whether enough information was provided by the user.

The agent's primary capabilities include:

  1. Retrieving Timesheet Data: Retrieving records from the SuccessFactor API containing the user's timesheet data.
  2. Logging work hours: Log works hours by posting records to the SuccessFactor API based on user input.

While there are other use cases that benefit more from agenticness, this demo serves the purpose of getting a good grasp of the core concepts of LangGraph by building a custom agent with human-in-the-loop control mechanisms.

Timesheet Management Agent: Overview

A demonstration of the agent's capabilities can be found here.

Core concept

As the name suggests, LangGraph is built around the idea of orchestrating language model workflows as a graph.
Each node can be understood as a unit designed for a specific tasks, such as a language model invocation or a tool execution. On the other hand, the edges define the flow of information. LangGraph ships a prebuilt react agent, broadly following the paper ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models (Yao et al., 2022). The paper outlines a framework that integrates reasoning and acting in language models by interleaving reasoning with task-specific actions. This prebuilt agent defines an agent and a tool node. The agent node handles LLM execution. The language model decides what tools to execute. If tool calls are present in the language model response, the execution is routed to the tools node where the tools are executed. Otherwise the agent finishes executions.
Generally, the agent iteratively executes multiple tools until it retrieved enough information or executed all relevant actions.

Our Agent architecture

We adapt the prebuilt react agent slightly by adding a review node enabling human-in-the-loop interactions. This is crucial as we want to prompt the user for confirmation before executing mutative actions.

Implementation

In the following I will expand on the implementation of the agent. To keep it simple I will leave out the frontend implementation. However, if you are interested, you can find the code for the frontend in the Git repository. The adjustments that need to be made to our agent to integrate with the frontend as well as our tool definitions can be found in the appendix. All code in this demo can be found in this Git Repository.

Setup

Prerequisites

What you should bring:

  1. (Limited) Experience with Python 
  2. SAP Account
  3. Access to the Generative AI Hub and instance keys

Environment Setup

Retrieve API Key

The SuccessFactors API we will use, comes with a preconfigured sandbox environment. To have access to this sandbox you have to login with your SAP Account and retrieve your API Key from the Payroll Time Sheet Page.
With the API Key set this environment variable or initialize the variable with your API Key in the Python file containing the tools:

  • PAYROLL_API_KEY: This is the API key you retrieved.

In a production environment you would, of course, retrieve your actual API Key.

Configure SAP AI Core

Follow all the steps under Initial Setup to configure your SAP AI Core, if you have not already done so.
With your service key follow the configuration section in this Documentation to set the following environment variables:

  • AICORE_CLIENT_ID: This represents the client ID.
  • AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET: This stands for the client secret.
  • AICORE_AUTH_URL: This is the URL used to retrieve a token using the client ID and secret.
  • AICORE_BASE_URL: This is the URL of the service (with suffix /v2).
  • AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP: This represents the resource group that should be used. (The standard resource group is "default")

Install python dependencies

Finally, you will need to install the required Python packages with a package manager of your choice.

pip install "langchain", "langgraph", "generative-ai-hub-sdk[all]"

For the frontend (Optional)

pip install "streamlit"

Initializing the LLM

First, we need to initialize the large language model the agent will use to perform actions and respond to the users request. Here we use gpt-4o as the underlying language model, a maximum token count of 1024 and a temperature of zero to minimize uncertainty. A temperature of zero will lead to more consistent results. However, the model output is not absolutely deterministic as the underlying calculations are inherently indeterministic.

Note: In order to initialize the language model the relevant environment variables need to be set (see Setup).

After initializing the LLM, we need give the agent access to the necessary tools.
We import three functions. get_records, post_records, and get_today from the time_tools module. These functions interact with the SuccessFactors Employee Central API:

  • get_records: Retrieves existing timesheet entries.
  • post_records: Submits new timesheet entries.
  • get_today: Fetches the current date.

The definition of these tools can be found in the appendix.

We bind the tools to the LLM. This allows the model to specify tool calls when it deems relevant.
For a more detailed view on the tools see the appendix.

Spoiler
import uuid
from typing import cast, Literal
from IPython.core.display_functions import display
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain import init_llm
from langchain_core.messages import AIMessage, ToolMessage
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate, MessagesPlaceholder
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableConfig
from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver
from langgraph.constants import START, END
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph
from langgraph.prebuilt import ToolNode
from langgraph.prebuilt.chat_agent_executor import AgentState
from langgraph.types import interrupt, Command, Send
from pydantic import Field, BaseModel
llm = init_llm('gpt-4o', max_tokens=1024, temperature=0)

from time_tools import get_records, post_records, get_today

agent_tools = [get_records, post_records, get_today]
agent_llm = llm.bind_tools(agent_tools)

Creating a system prompt

In order to ensure consistent and useful behaviour we need to define a well structured system prompt next.
Generally, you should:

  1. Define the role and persona.
  2. Establish context and objectives
  3. Outline clear instructions and constraints
  4. Provide examples of ideal responses (Optional)

By utilizing few-shot prompting model performance can be hugely improved. It also makes sense to encourage iterative clarification.

system_prompt ="""
Role and Objective:

  - You are a helpful AI Agent dedicated to assisting users with their timesheet management.
  - Your primary tasks include retrieving and posting timesheet data based on user requests.

Responsibilities:

  - Logging Work Time: Only log actual work time. Do not include any breaks.
  - Data Handling: When posting records, execute as many post_records calls in parallel as possible using the provided information.
  - Automatic Confirmation: When a post_records call is made, the user is automatically asked for confirmation over a GUI; do not prompt for confirmation.

Interaction Guidelines:

  - Language Consistency: Always respond in the same language as the user.
  - Transparency: Provide an explanation of what you are doing with every response.
  - Clarity and Accuracy:
    - If any part of the user’s request is ambiguous (for example, missing dates or unclear work times), ask clarifying questions rather than making assumptions.
    - Ensure all necessary details are provided before proceeding with any action.
"""

prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ('system', system_prompt),
    MessagesPlaceholder(variable_name='msg')
])

Defining the agent's nodes

Now we come to actually building our agent.
As previously outlined, our agent consists of three nodes:

  • the agent node: executing LLM calls,
  • the review node: prompting the user for confirmation before mutative action
  • the tool node: executing tools specified in the agent's response

When a node is called it is passed the graph state. We use the prebuilt AgentState which defines a variable messages by subclassing TypedDict. This is simply a list of messages which is passed to the language model at every invocation.

The agent node

In the agent node we simply call the language model, we initialized before, with the system prompt and the message history. We then update the graph state by returning the models response. The response is appended to the list of messages.
Each key in the graph state has its own reducer function. A reducer function defines how a variable in the graph state is updated. The prebuilt AgentState defines operator.add as the reducer for messages. Because of this returning [response] appends the list of messages with the response instead of overwriting it.

def agent(state: AgentState, config: RunnableConfig):
    model_input = prompt.invoke({'msg': state['messages']})
    response = cast(AIMessage, agent_llm.invoke(model_input, config))
    response.name = "agent"
    return {"messages": [response]}

The review node

Next we define the review node. For simplicity, we use only text input for verification. A more robust approach (used in the actual demo) can be found in the appendix. Here we use an additional language model for verification.
We use a workaround to get structured output from our model as of now structured_output is not supported. For this we bind a tool the model should use to structure its response.

class UserAffirmation(BaseModel):
    """Always use this tool to structure your response."""
    user_affirmation: bool = Field(description="Whether the user confirmed the action.")
    explanation: str = Field(description="An explanation of your decision.")

verification_llm = llm.bind_tools([UserAffirmation])

When control is passed to the review node, the post requests from the agent's response are fetched. If no post request is present, execution is resumed with the tools node. This is done by returning a Send object which routes execution. Otherwise the user is asked for confirmation with an interrupt. In contrast to Python's interrupt, execution is not resumed from the interrupt point, but rather the last node is executed again from top to bottom. This can be a common pitfall. When the user has supplied a response, we call a language model to process the user's input and retrieve the structured response. If the user approves we continue execution with the tool node. Otherwise, we update the state by appending a Tool Message and resume execution with the agent node. It is strictly necessary to append a Tool Message as most model providers require every tool call to be accompanied by a corresponding Tool Message.

from typing import Union


def human_review(state: AgentState) -> Command[Literal["agent", "tools"]]:
    last_message = state["messages"][-1]
    post_requests = [tool_call for tool_call in last_message.tool_calls if tool_call['name'] == 'post_records']

    if len(post_requests) > 0:
        confirmation_messages = [post_requests["args"]["confirmation_messages"] for post_reqeuest in post_requests]
        user_review = interrupt({"task": "Review the action.",
                           "action": confirmation_messages})
        
        output = verification_llm.invoke(
            [('user', user_review), ('system', 'Verify whether the user wants to continue with the action.')])
        should_continue = output.tool_calls[0]['args']['user_affirmation']
        print(f"Model explanation: {output.tool_calls[0]['args']['explanation']}")
        if should_continue:
            return Send(node='tools', arg=state)
        else:
            return Command(update={"messages": [ToolMessage('User did not confirm action.', tool_call_id=post_request['id']) for post_request in post_requests]}, goto='agent')


    else:
        return Send(node='tools', arg=state)

The tools node

For the tools node we use the prebuilt ToolNode. Here the tool calls are retrieved from the last message in the graphs message history and executed. For every tool call a Tool Message is appended to the message history containing the return value of the tool or an error message on failure.

tool_node = ToolNode(agent_tools)

Building the graph

Add nodes and edges

Now we build our graph by adding the nodes and edges. Edges define what nodes to execute next.

workflow = StateGraph(AgentState)

workflow.add_node('agent', agent)
workflow.add_node('tools', tool_node)
workflow.add_node('human_review', human_review)

workflow.add_edge(START, "agent")
workflow.add_edge("tools", "agent")


display(workflow.compile())

Add conditional edges

Conditional edges define what node to execute next based on a condition. We add a conditional edge which routes the execution from the agent node either to the review node or the end node depending on whether the language model executed a tool call.

def should_continue(state: AgentState) -> Literal["tools", "__end__"]:
    last_message = state["messages"][-1]
    if hasattr(last_message, "tool_calls") and len(last_message.tool_calls) > 0:
        return "tool call"
    return "__end__"

workflow.add_conditional_edges(source="agent", path=should_continue, path_map={"tool call": "human_review", "__end__": END})

Compile the graph

Finally, we compile our graph. When compiling we add a checkpointer to achieve thread-level persistence. With a checkpointer specified at compilation, a snapshot of the graph state is saved at every superstep. This is crucial for human-in-the-loop interactions as we need to resume execution after an interrupt is called.

checkpointer = MemorySaver()
timesheet_agent = workflow.compile(checkpointer=checkpointer)
display(timesheet_agent)

Invoke the agent

def process_output(stream):
    for token in stream: 
        (key, content), = token.items()
        if key == "__interrupt__":
           print(content[0])
           return True 
        if content is not None:
           content['messages'][-1].pretty_print()
    print("\n")
    return False

Now we can stream the output of our agent. We hand over a dictionary containing the user's input and a config with our thread id. Each thread represents an individual session between the graph and the user. If we want to continue the conversation, we need to pass the same thread id to the graph. As you can see we pass a Command object instead of a dictionary the second time we invoke the agent. This is necessary when an interrupt is triggered.

config = {"configurable": {"thread_id": str(uuid.uuid1())}}

user_input = {'messages': ['user', 'Today I worked from 6 to 6 with a half hour break at 12.']}
process_output(timesheet_agent.stream(user_input, config, stream_mode='updates'))

user_input = Command(resume='Sure.')
process_output(timesheet_agent.stream(user_input, config, stream_mode='updates'))
Spoiler
 ==================================User Input==================================
    Today I worked from 6 to 6 with a half hour break at 12.
    ==================================Ai Message==================================
    Name: agent
    
    To log your work time accurately, I will exclude the half-hour break from your total work hours. 
    
    Your work hours are:
    - From 06:00 to 12:00 (6 hours)
    - From 12:30 to 18:00 (5.5 hours)
    
    Total work hours: 11.5 hours
    
    I will now log these work hours for today. Let me first retrieve today's date.
    Tool Calls:
      get_today (call_rHtgYYzDZ5aTvTwyzf4oCmk1)
     Call ID: call_rHtgYYzDZ5aTvTwyzf4oCmk1
      Args:
    =================================Tool Message=================================
    Name: get_today
    
    2025-03-11
    ==================================Ai Message==================================
    Name: agent
    Tool Calls:
      post_records (call_uHlGJYSAiPqzwWkdBnoFudoh)
     Call ID: call_uHlGJYSAiPqzwWkdBnoFudoh
      Args:
        data: {'startDate': '2025-03-11', 'startTime': 'PT06H00M00S', 'endTime': 'PT12H00M00S'}
        confirmation_messages: Would you like to log your work hours from 06:00 to 12:00 on March 11th 2025?
      post_records (call_0KBLAkU10fyGTnUxPjRlFSdm)
     Call ID: call_0KBLAkU10fyGTnUxPjRlFSdm
      Args:
        data: {'startDate': '2025-03-11', 'startTime': 'PT12H30M00S', 'endTime': 'PT18H00M00S'}
        confirmation_messages: Would you like to log your work hours from 12:30 to 18:00 on March 11th 2025?
    Interrupt message: ['Would you like to log your work hours from 06:00 to 12:00 on March 11th 2025?', 'Would you like to log your work hours from 12:30 to 18:00 on March 11th 2025?']
    ==================================User Input==================================
    Sure.
    Model explanation: The user has confirmed the action by saying 'Sure.'
    =================================Tool Message=================================
    Name: post_records
    
    Entity created succesfully.
    ==================================Ai Message==================================
    Name: agent
    
    Your work hours for today, March 11th, 2025, have been successfully logged:
    - From 06:00 to 12:00
    - From 12:30 to 18:00
    
    If you need any further assistance, feel free to ask!

Now you can try interacting with the agent.

config = {"configurable": {"thread_id": str(uuid.uuid1())}}
interrupted = False

print("Type to interact with the agent (type q to quit):\n")
while True:
    user_input = input()
    if user_input.lower() == 'q':
        break
    print(user_input)

    if interrupted:
        interrupted = False
        user_input = Command(resume=user_input)
    else:
        user_input = {'messages': ['user', user_input]}

    interrupted = process_output(timesheet_agent.stream(user_input, config, stream_mode="updates"))
Spoiler
 Type to interact with the agent (type q to quit):
    ==================================User Input==================================
    worked from 10 to 11
    ==================================Ai Message==================================
    Name: agent
    
    Please provide the date on which you worked from 10:00 to 11:00. This will help me log your work time accurately.
    
    
    ==================================User Input==================================
    yesterday
    ==================================Ai Message==================================
    Name: agent
    Tool Calls:
      get_today (call_dtYYuTiZKB6TI8OIhtQSyMbZ)
     Call ID: call_dtYYuTiZKB6TI8OIhtQSyMbZ
      Args:
    =================================Tool Message=================================
    Name: get_today
    
    2025-03-11
    ==================================Ai Message==================================
    Name: agent
    
    I will log your work time from 10:00 to 11:00 on 2025-03-10 (yesterday).
    
    Proceeding to post the record.
    Tool Calls:
      post_records (call_P6bfNYrinCaR4tjLdetMNEjP)
     Call ID: call_P6bfNYrinCaR4tjLdetMNEjP
      Args:
        data: {'startDate': '2025-03-10', 'startTime': 'PT10H00M00S', 'endTime': 'PT11H00M00S'}
        confirmation_messages: Would you like to log your work hours from 10:00 to 11:00 on March 10th, 2025?
    Interrupt: ['Would you like to log your work hours from 10:00 to 11:00 on March 10th, 2025?']
    ==================================User Input==================================
    sounds good
    Model explanation: The user confirmed that the action sounds good.
    =================================Tool Message=================================
    Name: post_records
    
    Entity created succesfully.
    ==================================Ai Message==================================
    Name: agent
    
    Your work time from 10:00 to 11:00 on March 10th, 2025 has been successfully logged. If you need any further assistance, feel free to ask!

Implementing your own agent

Now you have all the knowledge to build your first own agent. But before you start implementing your own llm-based agent, you need to assess whether your specific use case benefits from the capabilities of agentic AI or if a more traditional approach might be more fit. In general, agents unleash their full potential in scenarios where multistep "human-like-reasoning" is required especially when subsequent steps need to be chosen dynamically.

As you might have noticed the use case of a timesheet management agent doesn't fully exhibit these properties. There are other use cases that benefit more from agenticness. Here you can find a video of a use case that highly benefits from agentic AI. This example shows an agent for exploratory data analysis, insight generation and sales forecasting, purely built with the ReAct Agent LangGraph ships out of the box.

Outlook

In this blog we explored building a human-in-the-loop agent for timesheet management using the SAP Generative AI Hub SDK, LangGraph and a SuccessFactors API. By walking through a simple yet customizable example, we demonstrated how approachable and powerful agentic workflows can be. While our demo focused on a straightforward use case, it provides the foundational knowledge necessary for you to expand into more complex, highly agentic use cases such as insight generation, automated customer service or intelligent procurement.
By integrating these tools into your workflow today, you can stay ahead of the curve as llm-based agents will become the cornerstone of business automation.
Now it's your turn!

Appendix

Defining the tools

Spoiler
import urllib.parse
import uuid
from datetime import date, datetime
from typing import TypedDict, Annotated
import requests
from langchain_core.tools import tool, InjectedToolCallId

Define headers and API endpoint.

os.environ["PAYROLL_API_KEY"] = "<YOUR API KEY>"
API_KEY = os.environ.get("PAYROLL_API_KEY")

get_header = {
    'Accept': 'application/json',
    'APIKey': API_KEY,
    "DataServiceVersion": "2.0"
}

post_header = {
    'Content-Type': 'application/json',
    'APIKey': API_KEY,
    "DataServiceVersion": "2.0"
}

base_url = 'https://sandbox.api.sap.com/successfactors/odata/v2/'
#randomly chosen user_id
user_id = '100257'

Now we define a class containing all relevant information to make a post request to the API endpoint. This is the class the LLM will use to pass data to our tool making post requests.

class ExternalTimeData(TypedDict):
    startDate: str
    startTime: str
    endTime: str

Next we define our tools. A tool can be any Python function. You should always provide a meaningful docstring as this is what the LLM will use to infer the tools functionality. This first tool retrieves today's date.

@tool
def get_today():
    """Returns today's date."""
    return date.today()

This next tool retrieves entries of ExternalTimeData for the current user only.

@tool
def get_records(top:int):
    """Retrieve ExternalTimeData entries from the API for the current user.
    top: Specifies the number of entries to retrieve.
    """
    params = {}
    if top is not None:
        params['$top'] = top
    params['$filter'] = f'userId eq {user_id}'
    query_text = urllib.parse.urlencode(params, safe='(),')

    table = 'ExternalTimeData'
    url = f'{base_url}{table}?{query_text}'
    response = requests.get(url, headers=get_header)

    if response.status_code == 200:
        data = response.json()
        return data
    else:
        return f'Error: {response.content}'

Our last tool posts entries to ExternalTimeData. While these tools are rather simple, tools can be arbitrarily complex. In general, it's a good idea to only bind a handful of tools to one LLM. If you need more tools you should consider a multi-agent architecture or implement a tool selection based on the user's messages using embedding for instance.

@tool
def post_records(data: ExternalTimeData, confirmation_message : str):
    """Post work time records to the API.
    The user is first asked for confirmation.
    Changes by the user to the times to post are returned in the ToolMessage.

        Args:
            data:
                startDate: YYYY-mm-dd
                startTime/endTime: ISO 8601 e.g. 'PT09H00M00S'/'PT18H30M00S' (9:00-18:30). The time frame must only include work time.
            confirmation_message: Confirmation message of the action containing a detailed summary.
            Include the specific date and time e.g. Confirm work time from 11:00 to 14:00 on June 15th 2025.
        """

    payload = dict(data)
    payload['startDate'] = f"/Date({int(datetime.fromisoformat(data['startDate']).timestamp()) * 1000})/"
    payload['externalCode'] = str(uuid.uuid1())
    payload['userId'] = user_id
    payload["userIdNav"] = {
        "__metadata": {
            "uri": f"https://sandbox.api.sap.com/successfactors/odata/v2/User('{user_id}')"
        }
    }

    table = 'ExternalTimeData'
    url = f'{base_url}{table}'

    response = requests.post(url, headers=post_header, json=payload)

    if response.status_code in (200, 201):
        return f'Entity created successfully: {data}'
    else:
        return  f'Error creating entity ({data}):  {response.status_code}: {response.content}'

Implementing the frontend

The frontend is implemented using the opensource framework Streamlit. The code can be found in the Git repository. Navigate to the folder containing the full example code and run the following command to start the application:

streamlit run frontend.py

For the implementation we will adjust the implementation of the review node. Similar to before, an interrupt is triggered when the agent node tries to make a post request. When this happens the user must provide explicit feedback for every action. We change the last message in the graph's message history to include all tool calls not directly approved and append ToolMessages accordingly. Lastly, we append a message with all approved action. This ensures a consistent message history as well as ensuring only approved actions are performed. The options the user can choose from are the following:

  • Approve: The request is routed to the tool node. The required ToolMessage is automatically created by the ToolNode.
  • Deny: Before routing the request to the ToolNode, we append a ToolMessage.
  • Make Changes: Similar to the previous option, a ToolMessage is appended including additional information on changes to the action. 
Spoiler
def human_review(state: AgentState):
    last_message = state["messages"][-1]
    post_requests = np.array(list(filter(lambda x: x['name'] == 'post_records', last_message.tool_calls)))

    if len(post_requests) > 0:
        confirmation_messages = list(map(lambda x: x['args']['confirmation_message'], post_requests))
        user_review = interrupt({"task": "Review the action.",
                           "action": confirmation_messages})

        user_review = json.loads(user_review)
        selections = np.array(user_review['selections'])
        user_changes = user_review['user_changes']

        approved = post_requests[selections == 0]
        approved_message = AIMessage(content=last_message.content, tool_calls=approved.tolist())

        not_approved = post_requests[selections != 0]
        not_approved_message = AIMessage(content=last_message.content, id=last_message.id, tool_calls=not_approved.tolist())

        denied = post_requests[selections == 1]
        to_change = post_requests[selections == 2]
        denied_messages = [ToolMessage(f"Tool call was not executed (denied by user): "
                                       f"{row['args']['confirmation_message']}."
                                       f"Do not post the same record again.",
                                       tool_call_id=row['id'])
                           for row in denied]

        to_change_messages = [ToolMessage(f"Tool call was not executed (user wants to make changes)."
                                          f"To this message: "
                                          f"<{row['args']['confirmation_message']}> "
                                          f"the user responded: "
                                          f"<{user_changes}>"
                                          f"Incorporate this request and call the tool again. "
                                          f"Do not call the tool with the same values.",
                                          tool_call_id=row['id'])
                           for row in to_change]

        tool_messages = denied_messages + to_change_messages

        return Command(update={
            "messages": [not_approved_message] + tool_messages + [approved_message]}, goto='tools')
    else:
        return Send(node='tools', arg=state)

Deploy the application with Cloud Foundry

In order to roll out the application you need to:

  1. Clone the Git repository
  2. Enter your credentials in manifest.yml
  3. Configure the Cloud Foundry CLI

After following these steps you can push the app with:

cf push

 

Thanks to @AndreasForster for his feedback and help! You can find a blog he wrote on implementing an invoice agent here.

entry.title: Yearly and Monthly Limit of Pay Component
entry.content:
Problem Statement:
The client requires a yearly cap to be implemented for the One-Time Pay Component, ensuring that the total amount per calendar year does not exceed $6,000 and each occurrence does not exceed $1,500. This cap may vary depending on the country.

There is no standard way to handle it in SAP SuccessFactors. We can put validation on each occurrence of Pay Component amount but not on yearly cap. I developed a custom solution using custom MDF object and CPI iFlow to meet the requirement.

How this requirement can be achieved:

This requirement can be achieved in 4 steps.

Create a Custom MDF Object to Store Yearly Cap per Country:

  • Since the yearly cap can differ across countries, a custom MDF object should be created to store the yearly cap for each country.
  • This custom MDF object should be created using “Configure Object Definition”.

Create a CPI iFlow:

  • Develop a CPI iFlow to update values from the Non-Recurring Pay Component Portlet to the custom MDF object as mentioned in step 3.

Create a Custom MDF Object for Sum of Approved Non-Recurring Pay Component Amounts for an employee:

  • Create another custom MDF object to store the sum of approved Non-Recurring Pay Component amounts for the Pay Component in a calendar year for each employee.

Create a Business Rule to Validate Yearly Cap:

  • Develop a business rule to validate the yearly cap whenever a Non-Recurring Pay Component entry is maintained for employees.

 

Create a custom MDF object to store yearly cap per country: Yearly cap can be different for different countries hence a custom MDF object is created to store yearly cap for countries.
Custom MDF Object should be created using “Configure Object Definition”.

 

 

Create a custom MDF object to store yearly cap per country:

 

 

 


Sample entry where Maximum Limit Per Instance for Impulsive Pay refers each time, one-time payment amount cannot be more than this for USA employees.
Maximum Limit Per Calendar Year for Impulsive Pay refers yearly cap for one-time payment amount.

Create a custom MDF object to store sum of approved Pay Component Amount in a calendar Year for employees:

 

 

 

 

 

Sample entry where field details are as given below:

Key: It is unique combination USERID_Year (to distinguish between each calendar year cumulation entry for the employee)

Employee: User ID of employee
Pay Component: Pay Component
Calendar Year: Calendar Year
Count of Impulsive Pay in a Calendar Year: Number of instances, the pay component is paid in a calendar year to the employee
Sum of Impulsive Pay Amounts in a Calendar Year: Sum of the pay component amount paid in a calendar year to the employee



 

 

Non Recurring Pay Component (One Time Payments) Portlet entry for an employee

 

 

 



There is a CPI iFlow to map value from Non Recurring Pay Component (One Time Payments) Portlet to Custom MDF Object Impulsive Pay Cumulation. Job is scheduled to run on daily basis to update custom MDF entries (Frequency can be changed based upon client requirement).

 

 

 

 

Note : In place of CPI iFlow, I created Integration centre job to update Custom MDF Object Impulsive Pay Cumulation but certain scenarios are not working on IC job (cumulation was not correct for back dated entries).

There are business rules to validate per instance pay component limit and yearly cap for pay component.


Per Instance Limit validation Rule

 

 

 

 

 

 

Yearly Cap Validation Business Rule

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Conclusion

Thank you for reading this blog. It is my first blog, your feedback, questions, and suggestions are always welcome. We’d love to hear about your experiences, custom requirements, or any insights you have, so please feel free to share them in the comments section below. Happy Learning 😊

entry.title: Deprecation of Fields in Job Information (1H 2025)
entry.content:
In the ever-evolving world of software development, maintaining a clean and efficient codebase is crucial. Just as we add new fields to accommodate new features, it's equally important to remove fields that no longer serve their intended purpose. This practice not only enhances the stability of an application but also ensures its maintainability.

In our ongoing efforts to maintain a robust application, we are deprecating and planning to eventually remove two legacy fields from Employee Central V1 from the Job Information entity. This process is guided by our comprehensive documentation, ensuring a smooth transition without disrupting existing functionalities.

Field
End of Development
End of Maintenance
Deleted
change-reasonMay 16, 2025May 16, 2025November 14, 2025
change-reason-externalMay 16, 2025May 16, 2025November 14, 2025

 

Note: 
These fields have nothing to do with the event-reason field, which is not impacted by this deprecation. The change-reason and change-reason-external fields were used in an older version of Employee Central and are no longer used for any purpose.

What you need to know: If you are using either of the fields mentioned above, you should plan to migrate them to a replacement object or custom field. Extract the data from Job Information and import it into the target fields.

Feel free to reach out and leave a comment below this blog if you have any questions or need further clarification on this process. Your feedback is always welcome!

entry.title: Why You Should Enable SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Quick Action Cards — And How to Configure
entry.content:
In today’s fast-paced work environment, your HR tools need to keep up with the pace of change. Whether it's employees updating their personal info or managers reshaping their teams, SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Quick Action Cards provide a fast, focused self-service experience right from the homepage.

This blog post walks you through why you should enable them, and how to configure the most impactful use cases for both employees and managers — including three powerful Employee (ESS) and Manager Self-Service (MSS) scenarios to get you started.Why Quick Action Cards Matter

Quick Action Cards simplify the most common HR tasks by making them immediately accessible, targeted, and role-specific. Whether it’s a name change or a team restructure, Quick Actions help users:

  • Complete transactions in fewer clicks: configure up to 8 fields per action, no clutter
  • Role-Based Display: Tailored views for employees vs. managers
  • Fast Configuration: No code needed, admin-friendly UI
  • Workflow-Ready: add approvals where needed
  • Keep records accurate and current

Future-Proof: Integrates with Joule, SAP’s AI copilot
And it’s all easily configurable in just a few clicks from the Admin Center.

👤For Employees: Quick & Intuitive Self-Updates

Here’s how to enable the 3 most common Employee Self-Service Quick Actions:

1. Change Legal Name
Base Object: Personal Information
Fields to Include:

  • First Name
  • Middle Name
  • Last Name

Workflow: Yes – typically routed to HR admin for verification
Notes: If legal name changes require document uploads, attachment field support is coming in the next release.

2. Change Preferred Name
Base Object: Personal Information
Fields to Include:

  • Preferred First Name
  • Preferred Last Name (custom field if needed)

Workflow: Optional — typically no approval needed
Custom Labels: You can rename this card to "Update Preferred Name" for clarity

3. Change Home Address
Base Object: Home Address
Fields: Automatically includes all configured address fields in your data model (e.g., Street, Zip, Country, etc.)
Workflow: Optional — depends on internal policy
Note: Fields can't be configured individually; all address-related fields will show based on country rules.

 👔 Manager Self-Service: Empower Managers to Act - instantly

Quick Action Cards aren’t just for employees. Managers can use them to keep org structures, team data, and positions up to date. Here are three of the most powerful Quick Actions for Manager Self-Service:

1. Promotion Without Salary Change
Base Object: Job Information
Fields:

  • Job Classification
  • Incentive Plan (or any other non-compensation field, up to 8 total)

Event/Event Reason:

  • Event: Promotion
  • Reason: Promotion Without Salary Change

Workflow: Yes — approval typically required
Limitation: Cannot combine Job Info + Compensation Info in one Quick Action
Use Case: When a job title changes, but pay remains unchanged (e.g., internal mobility)

2. Update "To Be Hired" Flag (Request for Replacement)
Base Object: Position Information
Fields: To Be Hired (set to Yes/No)
Workflow: Yes — request flows to HR or approvers
Use Case: When a team member leaves and the manager needs to flag the position for recruitment

Configuration Tips:

  • Rename Quick Action: “Request Replacement”
  • Use description text to clarify purpose for the manager
  • Keep the form clean — only 1–2 fields needed

3. Deactivate Position
Base Object: Position Information
Fields: Position Status (set to Inactive)
Workflow: Yes — usually multi-step approvals
Use Case: When a role is no longer needed and should be removed from active headcount planning
Note: Even with multiple workflow steps, only the first step displays in the Quick Action interface for simplicity — full steps execute in the background. 

How to Configure These Quick Actions (Step-by-Step)

Here’s how easy it is to set up these Quick Action Cards:

Step 1: Navigate to Manage Data

  • Create New: Employee Central Quick Action Template
  • Select the relevant use case (e.g., “Promotion”)

Step 2: Set Up the Template

  • Name the Quick Action (e.g., “Promotion Without Pay Change”)
  • Choose fields (max 😎
  • Optionally set Event + Event Reason
  • Save

Step 3: Assign Role-Based Permissions

  • Go to Manage Permission Roles
  • Grant access to the Quick Actions under:
    • Employee on Self
    • Manager on Direct Reports
  • Ensure field-level edit permissions are enabled

Step 4: Test and Validate

  • Use Proxy mode to simulate employee or manager experiences
  • Check visibility, field behaviour, and workflow triggering

Tips for a Smooth Rollout

  • Pilot with a small group: Start with one region or department
  • Provide a how-to guide: Especially for name and address changes
  • Monitor adoption: Use admin reports to track usage
  • Communicate the "why": Focus on speed, simplicity, and empowerment

What's coming soon?*

SAP continues to expand the Quick Action capabilities. Here's what's on the horizon:

  • Attachment Field Support - add docs like legal proof

  • Marital Status Change use case
  • One-time Payment Quick Actions
  • AI via Joule - conversational self-service powered by these actions
  • Improved handling of country-specific fields

*Please refer to Product Roadmap Explorer for the most up to date information about SAP SuccessFactors Roadmap items. This post is for informational purposes only and does not represent a commitment or promise to deliver any features or functionality. Plans are subject to change at any time. 

Final Thoughts: Empowerment Starts with Simplicity

SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Quick Action Cards are not just UI enhancements, they’re tools that empower employees, unburden HR, and modernize the manager experience. With minimal setup and maximum impact, they’re the easiest way to boost your self-service strategy today.

Start with the use cases above — roll them out in phases, monitor adoption, and prepare your teams for a smarter, simpler future.

Additional Resources

Useful Links:

  • What’s New Viewer: Employee Central Quick Actions for Employee and Manager Self-Services | SAP Help Portal​
  • Employee Central Quick Actions | SAP Help Portal​
  • Use Cases for Employee Central Quick Actions | SAP Help Portal​
  • Configuring an Employee Central Quick Action Template | SAP Help Portal​
  • Differences Between Quick Actions and Self-Service Transactions | SAP Help Portal

Position Related Quick Actions:

  • New My Team Positions Page and Employee Central Quick Actions for Positions​ Employee Central Quick Actions for Positions | SAP Help Portal​
  • My Team Positions Page​
  • Permissions Required for Position Management in General

We are committed to support all of our SAP SuccessFactors customers on their individual Path to Success. These sessions are part of a wide range of ways we can support your evolution to continuous value and driving business outcomes.  

The original webinar & slides are available on-demand here:
https://play.goconsensus.com/u37a2d0be?fn=Muge&ln=Das&em= muge.das@sap.com&co=SAPSuccessFactors

#pathtosuccess #employeecentral #quickaction

entry.title: Job Information Manager Self-Service Now Displays Filtered Events Based on Event Reason Permissions
entry.content:
Job Information Manager Self-Service Now Displays Filtered Events Based on Event Reason Permissions

We're excited to share a new feature that significantly improves the user experience for managers using the "Change Job and Compensation Information" self-service transaction in Employee Central (EC). This enhancement addresses a highly voted feature request from our users (customer idea 48935), making the process more intuitive and efficient, particularly for customers who have set up their system to allow managers to manually select the Event and Event Reason.

The Challenge

Previously, managers faced a cluttered dropdown list of events, even if they didn't have permission for any of the associated event reasons. This often led to confusion and inefficiency when managers selected an Event and there was no single Event Reason available in the respective dropdown. In a nutshell, managers had to sift through irrelevant options.
Events displayed prior to the enhancement

The Solution

With our 1H 2025 update, we've introduced a filter to the Events dropdown list. Now, managers and admins will only see Events for which they have permission for at least one Event Reason which is active and valid for the selected date. This means that if a manager lacks permission for all event reasons tied to a specific event, that Event will no longer clutter their dropdown list. The same applies if there is no single active and valid Event Reason configured on the selected effective date.

Managers see Events only if they have permission for at least one associated Event Reason

How It Works

Let's break it down with an example:

Event Reasons Configured

There are many different Event Reasons configured in the system, usually several for each Event.

  • Data Change - Local Job Title (associated with Event Data Change)
  • Promotion - Pay Change (associated with Event Promotion)
  • Promotion - No Pay Change (associated with Event Promotion)
  • Transfer (associated with Event Transfer)
  • …

Some Event Reasons set up in the system

 

Manager's Permissions to Event Reasons

Managers typically have access to a limited set of Event Reasons, focusing on those essential for performing their duties effectively. Permissions control this access.

  • Data Change - Local Job Title
  • Promotion - No Pay Change 

Manager has permissions for 'Event Reasons Data Change - Local Job Title' and 'Promotion - No Pay Change' but does not have permissions for 'Promotion - Pay Change' and 'TRANSFER'

Events available in the Manager Self-Service Change Job and Compensation Information

When this manager accesses the Change Job and Compensation Information page, they will only see the following events in the dropdown:

  • Promotion (because they have permission for the Event Reason Promotion - No Pay Change, which is associated with this Event)
  • Data Change (because they have permission for the Event Reason Data Change - Local Job Title, which is associated with this Event)

Managers see Events only if they have permission for at least one associated Event Reason

 

Events like Transfer are excluded because the manager lacks permission for any associated Event Reason. This helps them focus on relevant options. This functionality is also mirrored in the Job Information History, ensuring consistency across the different UIs.

By optimizing the dropdown list, we are committed to elevating productivity and empowering managers to concentrate on their core responsibilities with greater efficiency. This enhancement is inspired by customer idea 48935 - Events should not appear if User does not have permissions to any associated event reasons.

 

Note: With the H1 2025 release, the new feature becomes available when managers first select Job Information in the transaction. However, if managers select Compensation Information before Job Information during this manager self-service transaction, they can view Events including those without an associated Event Reason for which they have permission. This gap is scheduled for a separate enhancement in a future release. For more details plese refer to https://me.sap.com/notes/0003586228. 

We welcome any questions or feedback; please leave a comment below this blog.

entry.title: Employee Central Check Tool Check for Job Information: Each user's history starts with a hire record
entry.content:
The event and employment status fields in Job Information are two of the most relevant fields across all of the Employee Central Core master data, because they represent what stage of the employment lifecycle the employee is in, and they control what can be done with the employee at which point in time.

The guardrails and additional validations that we introduced with Centralised Services ensure more consistent and reliable employee data. However, various customers still have inconsistent, older data in their systems that was created pre-Centralised Services, such as employees who do not have a hire record at all. We have had multiple requests to provide an easy way for customers to fix the issue of missing hire records.

With the 1H2025 release, we are introducing a new Check Tool Check which returns a list of all users without a hire record and contains a Quick Fix which modifies the first record in each of those users' Job Information history and updates the event reason with a "Hire" event reason that can be selected in the check. The check, including the quick fix, is available in category Validation (Employee Central Core - Job Information).

The check has two parameters: Company and Event Reason.

If there is no association defined between event reason and country, the check and quick fix can be run for all impacted users, so you can leave the Company field blank. The Event Reason value help returns all “Hire” event reasons. This parameter is required because this is the event reason which will be used to update the impacted users’ data in the quick fix.

If there is an association between event reason and country, and you have “Hire” event reasons which are not associated to all countries, run the check separately for each company. The hire event reason you select must be one that is associated to the selected company’s country/region of registration, otherwise the check will return a validation error.

The check will now find all users without a hire record (if a company was selected, only for this company).

The quick fix will always be executed on all users in the results list. In addition to the event reason, it also updates the event in those records with the standard “Hire” event. It will not update the employment status in case a user had no status or had an incorrect status, because it would then need to process the user’s full Job Info History and propagate the status to future records until the next status-changing event, making it less performant.

The quick fix will also create a Job Information audit record to document the field changes. It will not update the "last modified/last updated" details; these will remain unchanged.

In case the results list contains users whose employment status in their very first Job Information record is blank or anything other than "Active", an additional step is required to completely fix their data. After executing the quick fix, go to the Job Info History for each of these users via their employee profile. Select their hire record, change any unrelated field (e.g. “Notes”), and save. This will derive the correct employment status in the hire record and also forward propagate it correctly.

This enhancement is inspired by the customer idea 311185.

entry.title: Boosting HR Productivity with Employee and Manager Self-Service
entry.content:
In today’s evolving workplace, HR teams face increasing demands to streamline processes, improve data accuracy, and enhance employee experiences. One of the most effective ways to achieve this is through Employee and Manager Self-Service (ESS & MSS) functionalities in SAP SuccessFactors (SF). By enabling employees and managers to perform routine HR tasks independently, organizations can reduce administrative burdens, ensure compliance, and improve HR efficiency.

Key Considerations for Implementing Self-Service in Your Organization 

 

 

 

1. Technology Considerations

  • System Access & Availability: Ensure all employees, including deskless workers, have digital access to SAP SF via desktop kiosks, mobile apps, or Microsoft Teams integration.
  • Integration with Other HR Systems: Ensure Employee Central integrates seamlessly with payroll, performance, and learning modules to maintain data consistency.
  • User Experience (UX) Optimization: Configure Quick Actions, role-based permissions, and SAP Work Zone widgets for an intuitive and frictionless user experience.

2. Process Considerations

  • Identify Key HR Transactions: Prioritize high-impact processes like employee data updates, time-off requests, and manager-initiated promotions for self-service automation.
  • Optimize Workflows: Review approval chains, business rules, and validation checks to ensure streamlined yet compliant self-service transactions.
  • Ensure Data Accuracy: Implement data validation rules and audit logs to maintain integrity and compliance with HR regulations.

3. Policy Considerations 

  • Self-Service Governance: Define who can access, edit, and approve HR transactions to align with internal policies.
  • Security & Compliance: Ensure compliance with GDPR, ISO 27001, and SOC 2 security standards for employee data privacy.
  • Change Management Strategy: Proactively address resistance by engaging employees, providing training, and communicating the benefits of self-service adoption.

4. Resource Considerations 

  • HR & IT Collaboration: Ensure HR and IT teams work together to configure Quick Actions, test integrations, and support self-service adoption.
  • Training & Support: Use SAP WalkMe for in-app guidance, FAQ sections, and training materials to assist employees and managers in navigating self-service functionalities.
  • Continuous Improvement: Regularly assess self-service adoption, collect user feedback, and implement improvements based on analytics insights.

How SAP SuccessFactors Empowers ESS & MSS

SAP SuccessFactors provides a centralized, cloud-based platform where employees and managers can access and update HR data anytime, anywhere. The key functionalities include:

Employees can manage their personal records efficiently, eliminating dependency on HR teams for routine tasks:

Managers gain visibility into their team’s data and can execute HR-related actions directly through the system:

ESS & MSS Accessibility Across Platforms

SAP SuccessFactors ensures a seamless multi-platform experience, making self-service accessible:

  •  Web-Based Portal: Employees and managers can access SuccessFactors via a standard browser with a user-friendly UI.
  • Mobile App: The SAP SF Mobile App (iOS & Android) allows users to complete transactions anytime, anywhere.
  •  Microsoft Teams Integration: Quick Actions and HR requests can be initiated directly within Teams.
  • SAP Work Zone: Provides a digital workspace with HR dashboards, alerts, and self-service actions.

Optimizing ESS & MSS with Quick Action Cards

One of the most powerful innovations within SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central is Quick Action Cards — a streamlined, user-friendly way to execute HR transactions instantly.

What Are Quick Action Cards?

Quick Actions are pre-configured, simplified workflows that enable employees and managers to initiate and complete HR tasks with fewer clicks. They appear on the SAP SF homepage, reducing navigation complexity and increasing efficiency.

Use Cases for Quick Actions:

  •  Employee Quick Actions:
    • Update Address – Employees can change their address instantly without navigating complex forms.
    • Edit Banking Information – Modify payroll details securely.
    • Submit Time-Off Requests – Request leave in just a few clicks.
  • Manager Quick Actions:
    • Promote an Employee – Quickly initiate a promotion request, select new job details, and submit for approval.
    • Create a New Position – Duplicate existing roles or create a new job position with minimal input.
    • Change Employee Job Info – Modify direct reports' job titles, pay grades, or work locations.

Integration of Quick Actions with AI-Powered Chatbots

SAP’s Joule AI companion integrates with Quick Actions, allowing users to execute HR transactions via chat. For example, a manager can simply ask Joule to "promote Jada", and the system will prompt relevant fields, apply default settings, and initiate approval workflows.

The Business Case: Realized Benefits & ROI

Organizations that adopt SAP SuccessFactors ESS & MSS with Quick Actions report:

  • 50-75% reduction in HR administrative workload – by shifting transactional tasks to employees and managers
  • Time savings of 3-5 minutes per transaction – enabling HR teams to focus on strategic initiatives
  • Significant cost savings – one case study showed a mid-sized enterprise saved over $1.5M annually by optimizing manager workflows

Best Practices for Implementing ESS & MSS with Quick Actions

  • Assess Your Current Maturity Level – Determine how much self-service is already in use and identify gaps where automation can drive efficiency.
  • Make Transactions Easy to Find – Use homepage Quick Actions, SAP Work Zone, or mobile-friendly configurations to ensure employees and managers can quickly access key tasks.
  • Provide User-Friendly Navigation – Leverage role-based permissions to present only relevant tasks to different user groups, simplifying the interface.
  • Empower with Mobile Accessibility – Ensure all ESS & MSS features are available on the SAP SuccessFactors Mobile App to cater to deskless workers. Leverage AI for Smarter Workflows – Integrate Joule AI companion and Quick Actions for seamless task execution.
  • Monitor and Optimize – Use People Analytics to track usage, completion rates, and process efficiency to continuously improve the self-service experience.

Employee and Manager Self-Service is a game-changer for HR efficiency. With SAP SuccessFactors and Quick Action Cards, organizations can drive productivity, accuracy, and employee engagement, transforming HR from a reactive function into a strategic powerhouse.

Is your HR team ready to embrace the future of self-service? Now is the time to leverage SAP SuccessFactors to unlock efficiency and business value.

The original webinar & slides are available on-demand here: https://play.goconsensus.com/ued51434f?fn=Egle&ln=Pociute&em=egle.pociute@sap.com&co=SAPSuccessFactors

 

Additional Resources

Simplifying Employee and Manager Experiences with Employee Central Quick Actions

Boost Efficiency and Productivity by Activating AI Functionalities in Employee Central

Configuring SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Quick Actions

Using stories in People Analytics Templates

Use Cases for Employee Central Quick Actions Differences Between Quick Actions and Self-Service Transactions | SAP Help Portal

We are committed to support all of our SAP SuccessFactors customers on their individual Path to Success. These sessions are part of a wide range of ways we can support your evolution to continuous value and driving business outcomes.

entry.title: Why Maximizing Transactions in SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central is Critical
entry.content:
In today's dynamic business environment, HR teams are under increasing pressure to improve efficiency, ensure compliance, and provide seamless employee experiences. SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central (EC) serves as the foundation for modern HR management, and maximizing the number of HR transactions within EC is crucial to unlocking its full potential.

The Power of a Centralized HR System

Employee Central is designed to be the core HR system of record, meaning it should handle all key HR transactions, from employee data management to payroll, benefits, and time tracking. A well-configured EC system ensures that organizations move away from scattered, disconnected processes and embrace a unified, automated HR approach.

By consolidating HR transactions within EC, organizations can achieve:

✅ Operational Efficiency – automating and optimizing HR processes reduces administrative burden and allows HR teams to focus on strategic initiatives rather than manual tasks. This includes streamlining position management, improving employee record updates, and facilitating approvals with minimal human intervention. 

✅ Enhanced Compliance  – a centralized system ensures adherence to global and local labor laws, tax regulations, and company policies. Employee Central helps businesses avoid costly compliance errors by providing automated regulatory updates, audit trails, and built-in compliance frameworks.

✅ Reliable HR Data – Having a single source of truth ensures that workforce decisions are based on accurate and up-to-date data. Integrated reporting and analytics provide real-time workforce insights, allowing HR teams to proactively address challenges such as turnover rates, hiring trends, and workforce planning. 

Value Realization: Beyond Configuration

 

The journey to maximizing value from SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central does not start with system configuration—it starts with redefining business processes. Organizations moving from an on-premise system to the cloud must first consolidate and standardize their HR operations. This transition enables:

  • Stronger Alignment with Business Strategy – A well-defined HR process framework ensures that Employee Central is tailored to the company's goals and workforce structure.
  • Process Standardization Across Regions & Departments – Eliminating fragmented, localized processes and ensuring consistency in HR operations.
  • Data-Driven Decision Making – Establishing a clean and accurate HR data foundation before system configuration.

Once business processes are optimized, they are translated into technology through Employee Central. The system then becomes an enabler of efficiency, allowing HR teams, managers, and employees to leverage it as part of their daily workflow. The real value is not just in configuring EC—it is in ensuring that employees and managers actively use it for HR transactions, self-service, and decision-making.

Key HR Transactions to Manage in Employee Central

Managing HR transactions efficiently within EC ensures seamless workflows and better HR service delivery. Some key HR transactions that should be handled within EC include:

  • Position Creation & Management – Automating position creation improves organizational agility by reducing administrative workload for managers. Employee Central integrates position management with recruiting and succession planning, ensuring continuity and proper workforce planning.
  • Workforce Transactions – Handling promotions, transfers, terminations, and personal data updates efficiently. With EC, employees and managers can easily make changes through self-service functionalities, reducing the need for HR intervention.
  • Payroll & Benefits Integration – Ensuring accurate and timely payroll processing by connecting EC with payroll and benefits systems. This eliminates errors caused by data inconsistencies and ensures employees receive their salaries and benefits without delays.
  • Time & Attendance Management – Managing leave requests, work schedules, and time tracking within EC creates a seamless connection between employee work hours and payroll processing. Employee Central's time management solutions help organizations maintain labor law compliance and optimize workforce productivity.

Recent Innovations Enhancing HR Transactions

SAP continues to invest in Employee Central, embedding AI and automation to improve HR efficiency. Recent innovations include:

  • AI-Assisted Writing for HR Processes – Automating job descriptions and policy documentation.
  • Enhanced Organizational Charts – Providing a more intuitive, dynamic view of company hierarchies.
  • Quick Actions for Managing Team Positions – Allowing HR teams and managers to initiate position changes more easily.
  • Mobile-Optimized Employee Self-Service – Enabling employees to update personal details, submit leave requests, and manage HR transactions on the go.

Best Practices for Maximizing Employee Central Usage

To get the most out of Employee Central, organizations should:

  • Conduct Regular System Audits – Identify gaps in system usage and optimize configurations to align with business needs.
  • Train HR Teams & Employees – Provide continuous training to ensure users can fully leverage EC’s capabilities.
  • Utilize Adoption Insights – Leverage SAP’s adoption dashboards to analyze trends and improve HR process efficiency.
  • Integrate with Other SAP Solutions – Ensure EC is fully integrated with payroll, finance, and other enterprise applications for a seamless digital HR ecosystem.

Next Steps

SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central is more than just a system of record—it’s a system of action. Ensure you are making the most of it to drive efficiency, compliance, and workforce satisfaction.

If your organization is not yet maximizing Employee Central for HR transactions, now is the time to act. By increasing adoption and usage of EC’s capabilities, HR teams can drive measurable value across the organization.

To get inspiration on where to start, we have recorded a webinar as part of the Supercharge your HR with SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central programme. 

The recording on-demand and the more detailed presentation is available on-demand here: https://play.goconsensus.com/a2682b296?fn=Egle&ln=Pociute&em=egle.pociute@sap.com&co=SAPSuccessFactors

Additional Information 

SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central has a few powerful features that drive HR transactions and we've published a couple of blog posts that you may also find useful:

  • Boosting HR Productivity with Employee & Manager Self-Services
  • Quick guide to actually configuring Quick Action Cards in SAP SF EC

Other useful links

  • Architectural Leading Practices
  • Best Practices
  • HR Process Library
  • Leading Practices Framework
  • Latest Release & Roadmap
  • Empowerment Center

entry.title: List of Employee Central & Employee Central Payroll Guide Updates for the 1H 2025 Release
entry.content:
In SAP SuccessFactors User Assistance, we’ve been working on improving your information experience.

Qualtrics Surveys

On the Help Portal page for both the 2H 2024 and 1H 2025 versions, we have a Qualtrics Survey available.  The survey is specifically to ask your feedback about the Employee Central Workflows guide as well as the Managing Employment Lifecycle guide. We’d really appreciate a few minutes of your time to get your thoughts. Thank you!

In addition, we also have a Qualtrics survey available to get your feedback on the 2H 2024 version of the Data Model guide.  This is available on the SAP SuccessFactors Platform Help Portal page.

New Guide for Position Budgeting Control

We have added the Implementing Position Budgeting Control for Cloud guide. Position Budgeting Control for Cloud allows you to ensure that positions and employees are sufficiently budgeted for a stipulated financial period.

Data Model Guide Changes

On the Discover tab of the Employee Central Help Portal page, we have added a new Getting Started section. This contains the updated data model guide, which includes everything you need to know about the different data models, foundation objects, Business Configuration UI as well as links to the field information for Employee Central and all the country/region-specific field information.  For more information, refer to Setting Up Data Models and Managing Configuration. Note that the Data Model guide is published on both the Employee Central and Platform pages.

Company Structure Overview

The Company Structure Overview guide is now merged with the Org Chart guide. This is part of an ongoing effort to help you find the information you need, in the place you expect it to be. This guide is now published on both the Employee Central and Platform Help Portal pages. For more information, refer to Implementing Company Organization Structures and Charts.

Translation

You may have also noticed that the SAP Help Portal content is now available in many languages. The content is generated by machine translation. If you find any errors or if you have an improvement suggestion, please leave us feedback.
